did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Machine Learning (ML) algorithms, specifically Naïve Bayes (NB), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Random Forest (RF), for skin cancer classification. Feature extraction was performed using Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) on 2357 dermoscopic images from the Kaggle/ISIC dataset. The NB model achieved 96.61% accuracy and was deployed in a real-time mobile app for skin cancer prediction.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a machine learning-based mobile application for real-time skin cancer prediction. It details specific methods including data collection from the Kaggle/ISIC dataset (2357 images), preprocessing with Median Filter and Discrete Wavelet Transform, and training of Naïve Bayes, LDA, and Random Forest classifiers. It reports original results (e.g., NB model accuracy of 96.61%) and describes the deployment of the app using MIT App Inventor. Although published in conference proceedings (I-SMAC 2023), it contains original technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Machine Learning and Cloud-based Mobile App for Real Time Skin Cancer Prediction
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on I-SMAC (IoT in Social, Mobile, Analytics and Cloud) (I-SMAC 2023)
brief_summary: This study addresses the early detection of skin cancer by developing a machine learning-based mobile application for real-time prediction. The researchers utilized a dataset of 2357 dermoscopic images from Kaggle/ISIC, covering seven skin cancer types including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. Preprocessing involved converting images to grayscale and applying a Median Filter, followed by feature extraction using Discrete Wavelet Transform. Three classifiers—Naïve Bayes, Linear Discriminant Analysis, and Random Forest—were evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, and F1 score. The Naïve Bayes model achieved the highest accuracy of 96.61% and was subsequently deployed in a real-time skin cancer detection app using MIT App Inventor.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (95 train images). Described as a noncancerous fibrous mass.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (438 train images). Described as a serious kind of skin cancer that produces melanin.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (77 train images). Described as a form of skin growth that is usually benign.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (357 train images). Medically known as a nevus (or nevi), described as innocuous clusters of pigmented cells.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (114 train images). Described as a lump or patch of rough, scaly skin caused by UV radiation.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (139 train images). Referred to as vascular tumor or blood vessel tumor.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (181 train images). Described as a kind of skin cancer that can cause serious complications.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (462 train images). Listed in the abstract as 'pigmented benign keratosis' and in Table 1 as 'Pigmented Benign Keratosis'.
Included in the Kaggle/ISIC dataset used for training (376 train images). Described as skin cancer starting in basal cells.

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Nevus, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer types using Naïve Bayes, Linear Discriminant Analysis, and Random Forest classifiers on dermoscopic images. The task involves categorizing images into specific disease labels (e.g., melanoma vs. non-melanoma, or specific lesion types like dermatofibroma, seborrheic keratosis, etc.) rather than predicting continuous values.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle/ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of 2357 dermoscopic images of cancerous and noncancerous skin conditions compiled by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and sourced via Kaggle. The dataset contains images grouped by specific skin cancer types.

use.training: Used to train Naïve Bayes, Linear Discriminant Analysis, and Random Forest classifiers after preprocessing (grayscale conversion, Median Filter) and feature extraction (Discrete Wavelet Transform).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the ML models using metrics such as accuracy, precision, TPR, TNR, and F1 score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, TPR, TNR, precision, F1 score) and a comparative analysis of these metrics across Naïve Bayes, Linear Discriminant Analysis, and Random Forest classifiers.

Kaggle/ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The Naïve Bayes model achieved the highest performance metrics among the three classifiers and was selected for deployment in the real-time mobile application.

architecture_used.main_model: Naïve Bayes (NB), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Random Forest (RF)

architecture_details.nb_details: Gaussian Naïve Bayes using Bayes' theorem and Gaussian probability density function
architecture_details.rf_details: Ensemble of decision trees using majority vote for classification

conclusions: The Naïve Bayes (NB) classifier achieved the highest performance among the tested models (NB, LDA, and Random Forest) for skin cancer classification, with an accuracy of 96.61%, TPR of 98.31%, TNR of 94.92%, precision of 95.08%, and F1 score of 96.67%.
The Random Forest (RF) model achieved an accuracy of 94.07%, while the Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) model achieved an accuracy of 93.22%.
The NB model was successfully deployed in a real-time skin cancer prediction mobile application using MIT App Inventor.
The study utilized a dataset of 2357 dermoscopic images from the Kaggle/ISIC repository, covering nine classes of skin conditions including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and nevus.
Feature extraction was performed using Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) after preprocessing images with grayscale conversion and a Median Filter.
The system aims to assist in the early detection of skin cancer, addressing the difficulty dermatologists face in distinguishing between different types of skin cancers based on initial appearance.
important_remarks: The study relies on a relatively small dataset of 2357 images from Kaggle/ISIC, with significant class imbalance (e.g., 462 images for Pigmented Benign Keratosis vs. 77 for Seborrheic Keratosis), which may bias model performance.
The paper does not report external validation; results are derived solely from the internal 80:20 train-test split of the Kaggle dataset, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.
No statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between Naïve Bayes, LDA, and Random Forest models.

The feature extraction method (Discrete Wavelet Transform) and classifiers (Naïve Bayes, LDA, Random Forest) are traditional machine learning techniques rather than deep learning, which may limit performance compared to state-of-the-art CNNs on complex dermoscopic features.
The mobile app deployment uses MIT App Inventor, which may raise concerns about computational efficiency, model optimization, and real-time inference latency on diverse mobile hardware.
The paper does not discuss potential data leakage risks or rigorous preprocessing protocols beyond grayscale conversion and Median Filter, which are minimal for dermoscopic image analysis.
Clinical relevance is limited as the app is a proof-of-concept tool without reported clinical trials, user studies, or comparison against dermatologist performance in a real-world setting.
